England's Mental Health Plan Aims for Prevention and Community Focus

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Centre for Young Lives

5,610 followers

3w
  • Report this post

This is a big moment for England’s mental health. For many years, we’ve been calling for a radical shift towards a cross-government approach with a focus on prevention, and we look forward to contributing to the plan’s success.  Our nation’s mental health has gone downhill over recent decades due to things like the Covid-19 pandemic and the legacy of austerity. But change is possible, and we are pleased that the government is taking action.   We will be advocating for the plan to be ambitious, evidence-led and with a real focus on those communities most at risk of poor mental health; and to address the social and economic factors that shape all our mental health.

Department of Health and Social Care

  • Helena Jensen
    3w
    • Report this post

    It's a very welcome development, and I'm happy to see the commitment to prevention and early intervention. However, I have a couple of misgivings. Qualms. Doubts. The first concerns prevention. I'm in favour, obviously. But how much sway and how much room for manoeuvre does the government actually have over many of the chief drivers of poor mental health? Or, just as pertinently, if many of the contributing factors are rooted in socioeconomic inequality, how high can mental health truly rank in the priorities that shape government decisions around this structural societal problem? Will addressing historically low wages form part of the mental health strategy, for example? The second concerns early intervention. In fact, every single one of us has a part to play here. A part to play in overcoming reluctance to seek help, on our own part and on the part of others. A part to play in overcoming the social stigma of mental health issues. A part to play in building a world where it's as natural to seek help for the first signs of a mental health challenge as it is to go to A&E when you first fall off a climbing frame. There can't be much early intervention without early acknowledgement on our part that we have a problem. And I think deep down we all know that the British are, shall we say, Not Terribly Good at that. My great hope, and my ambition, for The Wellcome Prize for Mental Health Science with Nature, which we launched in London on Thursday evening, is that we can help to bring about real change on that last point. #MindTheScience

      The government's call for a cross-government mental health strategy is the right move. Prevention is the right ambition.   We know prevention doesn't only happen in a waiting room. For most adults it can happen at work – in the conversations managers have, in the cultures organisations build, in whether people feel safe to say they're not okay.   One in five people in England are affected by a common mental health condition. Most of them are in employment. If this strategy is going to shift the dial on prevention, workplaces have to be part of the plan.   We've spent nearly two decades building the evidence and the infrastructure to make that happen. We'll be engaging with the call for evidence to make the case for employers – and we'd encourage our community to do the same.   The call for evidence closes 10 July. 📅   Learn more: https://lnkd.in/exJ7v-vM #MentalHealthAwarenessWeek #WorkplaceMentalHealth #MentalHealthStrategy

    We welcome the Department of Health and Social Care announcement of a new Mental Health Strategy. For too long, worsening mental health and wellbeing, particularly among children and young people, have been addressed through short-term, under-resourced efforts. A strategy spanning the whole of government offers a real opportunity to take a different approach. We hope the strategy will be a lever to ensure the incoming recommendations from the prevalence review will be properly implemented across government

    Too many people with poor mental health are getting help far too late. Shifting towards prevention, early intervention and support in communities is the right approach and must deliver the right support at the right time. This will only succeed if the government works together, linking health with education, housing and jobs. Occupational therapists are experts in mental health, taking a whole person approach and helping people build routines and take part in everyday life. The strategy must go further to tackle the practical factors shaping mental health, not just symptoms and diagnoses, and ensure occupational therapy expertise is embedded throughout. Read our statement to find out more about our response: https://lnkd.in/eEE7_uyD

    I’m really encouraged to see the government’s renewed focus on prevention and early intervention within the new 10-year mental health strategy for children and young people. As a former primary school teacher, and having worked with children for over 20 years, I saw first-hand the growing number of children as young as 4 struggling with anxiety, emotional overwhelm, dysregulation, and school-related stress — often communicated through behaviour long before children had the words to explain how they were feeling. That experience is one of the reasons I created Reset with Vix — a platform designed to support children’s emotional regulation, wellbeing, and resilience through practical, accessible nervous-system-informed tools for schools and families. I strongly believe early emotional support matters. Helping children develop regulation skills, emotional literacy, and safe supportive environments earlier could play a powerful role in reducing future mental health difficulties. It’s encouraging to see growing recognition that children’s mental health must become a shared priority across education, healthcare, families, and wider society. #ChildrensMentalHealth #EarlyIntervention #Education #EmotionalWellbeing #PrimaryEducation #WellbeingInSchools #ResetWithVix

    Also keen to see how a strategy could emerge to be inclusive and ‘flex’ in regard to the multiple factors that can contribute to difficulties with mental health… To acknowledge that a standard ‘mental health intervention’ is not always the answer for the individual/family/community concerned. What might support better mental health in the long run but might be coming at it with a different kind of support/intervention? For example, for a carer struggling with their mental health - this could be improved by more flexible working pattern through support with their employer, a school closer to them, better support for their child, support with women’s health issues, rehab for an injury getting in the way of returning to a valued activity, increasing sense of belonging, purpose, understanding their own neurodiversity…and so on. ‘Whole health Whole person Whole Life’ conversations and strategy 🙏 Department of Health and Social Care NHS England Surrey and Borders Partnership NHS Foundation Trust Surrey County Council Surrey and Sussex Healthcare NHS Trust #neughbourhoodteams #transformation

  • Neil Harris
    3w

    Great to see the Government’s newly announced cross‑government Mental Health Strategy, marking an important moment for our country. With demand for mental health support at record levels and millions affected mental health problems the shift toward prevention, early intervention and community‑based support is not only welcome, it is essential. At Sport in Mind, we see every day how early intervention is essential and how physical activity isn’t just a “nice to have” - it is a proven, life‑changing intervention that helps people stay well, recover faster and reconnect with their communities. When the strategy highlights the need to look beyond clinical settings - to schools, workplaces, voluntary organisations and local communities - it speaks directly about the work we’ve been doing for the last 15 years in partnership with multiple NHS trusts. The Government’s commitment to a more joined‑up, preventative approach is a step in the right direction. But as many have already said, delivery is what matters now. Investment must reach the grassroots, where early support can prevent crisis, reduce pressure on the NHS and transform lives long before someone reaches breaking point. This strategy is an opportunity to build a system that truly supports people to live active, fulfilling lives. Let’s make sure we seize it.

  • Dr Dorothy Frizelle
    3w
    • Report this post

    Dear Department of Health and Social Care This strategy will only succeed if it breaks the mental health vs physical health false dichotomy. You cannot effectively treat the mind in isolation from the body, nor the body in isolation from the mind. For this strategy to be truly transformative, the allocation of existing and additional resources/workforce must reflect three non-negotiables: 1️⃣ Parity of Esteem through Integration: Physical and mental health are two sides of the same coin. We need multidisciplinary teams and integrated pathways, not siloed services. 2️⃣ Resource Sensitivity: New funding must be directed toward integrated care pathways where mental health support is embedded within physical health services (and vice versa). 3️⃣ Workforce Capability: We need a workforce trained to recognize that a patient struggling with a chronic physical illness is at high risk for mental health decline, and they deserve proactive support, not an afterthought. Psychology workforce functionally integrated within hospital and community physical health services. True health is holistic and this new strategy MUST reflect that. #MentalHealthReform #HolisticHealth #NHS #IntegratedCare #HealthPolicy #ComplexCare #HealthcareInnovation #PhysicalAndMentalHealth #PsychologyMatters #ClinicalHealthPsychology
